D3 Chemistry L3 Apply Reference

Disperse Dyes — Polyester Needs Heat

non-ionic micro-particles dissolve into polyester chains only above ~130 °C; below the glass-transition the dye sits on the surface and rinses away

Polyester (PET) is hydrophobic and has no –OH groups for water-soluble dyes to grab. Disperse dyes are tiny non-ionic crystals dispersed in the bath. Above the polyester glass-transition (~80 °C — but commercially 130 °C high-pressure for good penetration) the polymer chains gain mobility, and the dye crystals dissolve into the bulk plastic. Cool the fibre and the dye is trapped.

Drag the bath temperature. Below ~80 °C the dye sits on the surface; near 130 °C the particles penetrate and the fibre colours deeply. The cross-section animation shows dye crystals diffusing inward.
